1: Superscription — baqqar (sheep breeder) is Amos' occupation; the same word describes his herd at 7:14 where he also claims to be a boqer (cattle herder). The two terms may indicate he was a rancher of mixed flocks. Tekoa lay in the Judean wilderness ~10 miles south of Jerusalem, in territory remote from the northern shrines he will condemn. The date formula ('in the days of Uzziah… and Jeroboam') spans two prosperous kingdoms; the earthquake (hara'ash) is archaeologically attested at Hazor ~760 BC and independently referenced at Zechariah 14:5. The book anchors itself historically with unusual precision.
2: The LORD roars — yish'ag YHWH: the lion-roar image opens the book and echoes at Amos 3:4, Amos 3:8. The roar comes from Tsiyon… Yerushalam, not from Bethel or Dan — Amos, from Judah, locates YHWH's legitimate dwelling in the south despite preaching in the north. Ve-avlu ne'ot haro'im (the shepherds' pastures wither in mourning) — avalu from 'aval (mourn, dry up); the word play: mourning and withering are the same verb. Carmel's drying summit (rosh hakarmel) signals a judgment so total it reaches even the most fertile coastland.
3: Three… and for four — the formula 'al-shloshat pish'ei X ve'al-arba'ah lo ashivennu is a numerical graded idiom (x, x+1) expressing maximal accumulation, not literal counting. Pasha' (crime, transgression) — the root denotes covenant-rebellion, breaking an established relationship; its use for foreign nations is significant since it implies they are accountable to a moral order even without Israel's explicit covenant. Lo ashivennu (I will not revoke it/turn it back) — the antecedent is judgment; the decree is irrevocable. Damascus is judged for doshan ba-charitsot habarzel et-hagi-ad (threshing Gilead with iron sledges) — agricultural implements as instruments of mass violence.
4: Fire on the house of Hazael — ve-shilachti esh (I will send fire) — the divine fire formula recurs in every oracle (vv7, 10, 12, 14; Amos 2:2, 5). Azalah (it will consume/devour) — kal perfect; fire is the standard covenant-curse for defeated cities (Deuteronomy 13:16). Armunot Ben-hadad (fortresses of Ben-hadad) — Ben-hadad is a dynastic name/title for Aramean kings; the palace-fortresses (armon) are symbols of royal power and the objects of judgment in every oracle.
5: Break the gate-bar of Damascus — shavar berich Dammeseq: berich (gate-bar, crossbar) is the wooden or metal beam that secured the city gate; breaking it = military defeat and open access to the enemy. Toshav biqat-Aven (ruler of the Valley of Aven) — Aven (nothingness/wickedness) may be a polemic alteration of the place name 'On.' Ve-galah 'am Aram Qirah — exile to Kir; this was fulfilled (2 Kings 16:9) and reverses Amos 9:7 which says Aram came from Kir.
6: Three crimes of Gaza — Gaza represents Philistia. The crime is galotam galut shelemah lehassigir le-Edom (they deported a whole/complete captive population to hand over to Edom) — galut shelemah (complete deportation; shelemah = wholeness; not leaving survivors) is a specific aggravation. The crime is slave-trading: not conquest for territorial expansion but selling human beings for profit. Edom appears as the buyer, anticipating the Edom oracle (Amos 1:11-12).
7: Fire on the wall of Gaza — the same fire formula; chomah Azzah (wall of Gaza) vs. the earlier bet Chaza'el (house of Hazael); in each oracle the fire-object varies: house, wall, fortresses. The judgment is calibrated to the symbol of each nation's power.
8: Cut off the ruler of Ashdod — yashav (ruler, inhabitant) used as a title. Four Philistine cities are named (Ashdod, Ashkelon, Ekron — Gath notably absent, perhaps already destroyed). Va-hashivoti yadi 'al-Eqron (I will turn my hand against Ekron) — hashiv yad (turn the hand) is a gesture of attack. Ve-avad she'erit pelishtim (the remnant of the Philistines will perish) — she'erit (remnant) ironically used: the saving remnant concept inverted; their remnant will be destroyed.
9: Three crimes of Tyre — same formula. Tyre's crime is higgiram galut shelemah le-Edom (they handed over a complete deportation to Edom) — same phrase as Gaza (Amos 1:6). The added aggravation: ve-lo zakeru brit achim (they did not remember the covenant of brotherhood). Brit achim is unique in the OT; it most likely refers to the Hiram-Solomon treaty (achim = brothers as treaty partners, 1 Kings 9:13). The commercial slave-trade violated a standing covenant relationship.
10: Fire on the wall of Tyre — the fire formula repeated; ve-akhlah armnoteiha (it will consume her fortresses). Tyre's commercial fortresses (armonot) were famous in antiquity; their consumption by fire was fulfilled in 332 BC by Alexander.
11: Three crimes of Edom — Edom's crime is described in psychological depth unusual in the oracle series: three parallel phrases. Radof va-cherev achiv (he hunted down his brother with the sword) — radaf (hunt, pursue relentlessly); achiv (his brother) = Jacob/Israel; the Esau-Jacob sibling relationship underlies the accusation. Ve-shiches rachamav — the MKT renders 'stifled all compassion'; shacat (corrupt, destroy) applied to rachamim (compassions, womb-feelings). Ve-tipposh le'olam appam (his anger maintained without end) — tipposh (retained, kept permanently) for sustained rage.
12: Fire on Teman — Teman was a major city in Edom's north; Bozrah (modern Buseirah) in the south. Both fortress-cities targeted, covering the nation's extent.
13: Three crimes of the Ammonites — the crime is described with the most graphic specificity: be-vaq'am haroth ha-Gil'ad (because they tore open [ripped] pregnant women of Gilead) — baqa' (cleave, split open) used for the atrocity of Elisha's prediction (2 Kings 8:12) and Hazael's actions. The purpose: lema'an harkhiv et-gevulam (in order to extend their territory) — territorial expansion as the motive for this atrocity against civilian non-combatants.
14: Fire on Rabbah — berir'at Rabbah (on the wall of Rabbah) — Ammon's capital. The judgment is amplified with the war scene: bithru'ah beyom milchamah (amid battle cries on the day of war) and bise'arah beyom suphah (a storm on the day of whirlwind) — the double-day intensification formula appears also in eschatological judgment texts.
15: Their king will go into exile — malcam — could be the king (proper noun) or Milcom, the Ammonite deity (Zephaniah 1:5; 1 Kings 11:5). The ambiguity is textually present: the king and the national deity are both exiled together with the officials (sarav). Exile of the deity in ANE thought was the ultimate defeat — YHWH overcomes both human and divine powers of Ammon.
